questions.text: I tend to miss the significance of pauses and silences in conversation.
I find it satisfying to maintain cohesion as the group moves forward.
I take time each evening to prepare for the next day.
I believe that life's best moments are the ones you didn't plan for.
I am the person others call when something needs to get decided.
I feel that the depth of my caring for others is something I would never want to diminish.
I am aware of the way seating arrangements reflect social dynamics.
I find that I am naturally oriented toward building my own life rather than supporting someone else's.
I tend to remain physically relaxed even when discussing difficult topics.
I believe that structured, rational engagement with emotionally difficult situations is the approach most likely to minimize harm and maximize benefit.
Even reading about an intense event can produce a strong physical reaction in me.
I feel that self-interest should always yield to the greater good.
I sense when someone is code-switching between their authentic self and a social persona.
I hold the view that reliability is one of the most important qualities a person can have.
I notice that even subtle shifts in atmosphere — a room going quiet, a change in tone — hit me with force.
I am the type who creates the rules of engagement for a group.
I believe that awareness of suffering creates a moral duty to act, not merely to acknowledge.
I often skip meals or sleep to help someone who is counting on me.
